title: Week 10: Systems Thinking and Agent-Based Modeling

yaml.title: Week 10: Systems Thinking and Agent-Based Modeling
yaml.subtitle: IA 240: Technology Applications in a Networked World

markdown.markdown:
::: {.callout-important} **1. Vi Hart & Nicky Case, ["Parable of the Polygons."](https://ncase.me/polygons/)** An interactive, playable model of Thomas Schelling's segregation dynamics: mildly biased individual "shapes" produce sharply segregated neighborhoods. A hands-on demonstration of how simple agent rules generate emergent, system-level patterns no individual intended. *As you read (and play), consider:* - Segregation emerges even though no single polygon "wants" it. How does that challenge the way analysts reason from individual intent to collective outcome? - If small biases compound into large patterns, where might an analyst mistake an emergent, system-level effect for deliberate coordination? **2. Donella Meadows, ["Leverage Points: Places to Intervene in a System,"](https://donellameadows.org/archives/leverage-points-places-to-intervene-in-a-system/) The Donella Meadows Project (1999).** The canonical short read on systems thinking — stocks, flows, feedback loops, and where a small change produces large effects. *As you read, consider:* - Meadows ranks paradigms and goals as higher-leverage than parameters. For a problem like insurgency or proliferation, where are analysts most tempted to intervene, and is that high- or low-leverage? - She warns that pushing the "obvious" lever often backfires. Can you think of an intelligence or policy fix that made the system worse? ::: ::: {.learning-objectives}
✓

Learning Objectives

By the end of this lecture, you should be able to:

- ::: # Systems Thinking ## What is Systems Thinking? Systems thinking is an analytical approach that views complex phenomena as *systems*—sets of interconnected, interdependent components whose interactions produce emergent behavior that cannot be understood by examining parts in isolation. Rather than asking "What causes X?", systems thinking asks "How do the relationships *among* multiple variables produce the pattern we observe in X?" ::: {.callout-note} ## Relevance to Hypothesis Development When you identify variables for a model, you are making implicit claims about what matters and what does not. Systems thinking forces you to be explicit about those claims—and to consider feedback loops and unintended consequences that purely linear thinking misses. ::: ## Key Principles of Systems Thinking ::: {.key-terms} ::: {dl} Interconnectedness : Every element of a system is connected to other elements through relationships. Changes in one variable propagate through the system. Analyzing any single variable in isolation produces an incomplete picture. Feedback Loops : Closed causal chains where an effect loops back to influence its own cause. There are two types (see below). Reinforcing (Positive) Feedback Loops : Change in one direction amplifies further change in the same direction. Not inherently positive in valence—can represent escalation, arms races, or cascading instability. Example: military spending increases → perceived threat increases → adversary military spending increases → perceived threat increases further. Balancing (Negative) Feedback Loops : System-level resistance to change; the loop pushes back against deviation from a target state. Example: high inflation → interest rate increases → borrowing decreases → economic activity slows → inflation decreases. Non-linearity : Relationships in complex systems are often non-linear—a small change in an input may have a large effect on an output (tipping points), or a large change may have negligible effect (thresholds). Linear models cannot capture these dynamics. Emergence : System-level properties that are not present in any individual component. The whole behaves differently than the sum of its parts. Terrorist network resilience is an emergent property—no single node produces it. Delayed Effects : Effects may manifest long after their cause. Sanctions imposed today may not produce intended economic effects for months or years. Models that ignore time lags will misattribute causation. Holistic View : Analysis must encompass the whole system, not just the most visible or accessible parts. Focusing only on measurable variables creates blind spots. ::: ::: --- # Part VIII: Causal Loop Diagrams ## Building a Causal Loop Diagram (CLD) A causal loop diagram is a visual representation of the causal relationships and feedback loops within a system. It does not quantify relationships—it maps their direction and structure. **Step-by-step process:** ::: {.timeline} ::: {dl} Step 1: Define the System : State the key problem or question you are exploring. What are the boundaries of the system? What outcomes are you trying to explain? Step 2: Identify Key Variables : List and categorize the variables relevant to your system. Are they independent, dependent, or intermediate? What scale and type are they? Step 3: Establish Causal Links : For each pair of related variables, draw an arrow from cause to effect. Label each arrow: **(+)** if an increase in the cause produces an increase in the effect; **(-)** if an increase in the cause produces a decrease in the effect. Step 4: Identify Feedback Loops : Trace closed loops in your diagram. Count the number of negative links: an **even number** (including zero) indicates a reinforcing loop; an **odd number** indicates a balancing loop. Step 5: Draw the Diagram : List all variables, then add arrows and signs. Clarity and legibility matter—a cluttered CLD is as useless as a cluttered chart. Step 6: Review and Adjust : Are there gaps? Are feedback loops internally consistent? Have you included all major drivers and consequences? Have independent reviewers examine the diagram. ::: ::: ## Benefits of Systems Thinking for Analysis ::: {.col-pair} ::: {.col-left} **Better Variable Identification** Systems thinking forces analysts to think beyond obvious first-order causes. A system diagram of "nuclear proliferation" might reveal that domestic politics, alliance commitments, and regime security concerns interact with the technical capability variables that tend to dominate quantitative models. **Enhanced Predictive Power** By mapping how variables interact—including delayed effects and feedback loops—analysts can anticipate how a change in one variable will ripple through the system. Interventions that appear effective in isolation may be counterproductive at the system level. ::: ::: {.col-right} **Better Decision-Making** Systems thinking highlights potential unintended consequences *before* they occur. Sanctions designed to reduce a regime's capability may strengthen its internal cohesion through a rally-around-the-flag effect—a balancing loop that counteracts the intended effect. **More Robust Hypotheses** Hypotheses derived from systems thinking are more likely to specify the *mechanism* connecting variables, not just the correlation. Mechanistic specificity is a hallmark of analytically rigorous hypothesis development. ::: ::: --- # Part IX: Real-World Systems Thinking Cases ## Case 1: Sanctions Against North Korea — A Feedback Loop Analysis Sanctions imposed on North Korea to compel denuclearization have operated for decades with limited observable effect on North Korea's weapons programs. Systems thinking explains why. The causal structure contains multiple feedback loops that counteract the intended effect of economic coercion. **Intended mechanism (the linear assumption):** > Sanctions → Economic pain → Regime capitulation → Denuclearization **What systems analysis reveals:** ::: {.col-pair} ::: {.col-left} **Reinforcing loop — Rally-around-the-flag** Sanctions-induced economic hardship → Regime frames hardship as foreign aggression → Domestic nationalism increases → Regime legitimacy strengthens → Hardline position becomes domestically necessary to sustain This reinforcing loop amplifies regime cohesion in direct response to the pressure intended to weaken it. Empirical studies on North Korea have found that periods of intensified sanctions correspond with increased domestic propaganda output, not capitulation signals. ::: ::: {.col-right} **Balancing loop — Elite defection risk** Sanctions → Economic contraction → Elite material wellbeing threatened → Elite defection or coup risk → Regime redistributes resources to core loyalists → Economic contraction among population deepens, but regime stability is maintained This balancing loop absorbs the potential political threat from sanctions by concentrating resources within the elite network—precisely the opposite of the intended distributional effect. ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note} ## Analytical Takeaway A linear model of sanctions predicts capitulation. A systems model predicts adaptation and consolidation—which is what the empirical record shows. Before recommending or evaluating a coercive policy instrument, mapping the full causal structure (including second- and third-order loops) is essential analytical due diligence. ::: ## Case 2: The 2003 Iraq Occupation — Institutional Collapse as a System Failure The postwar period in Iraq demonstrated how policy interventions can trigger cascading system failures through mechanisms that linear planning does not anticipate. **The critical decision:** Coalition Provisional Authority Order No. 2 (May 2003) disbanded the Iraqi Army, removing approximately 400,000 armed men from institutional employment overnight. **System consequences:** ::: {.timeline} ::: {dl} Phase 1: Initial shock : Army dissolution → immediate loss of income and status for 400,000 individuals with weapons training → security vacuum as the primary order-providing institution disappears Phase 2: Grievance amplification (reinforcing loop) : Unemployed soldiers → economic desperation + institutional grievance → insurgent recruitment becomes easy and cheap → insurgency capability increases Phase 3: Sectarian activation : Security vacuum → Shia militias and Kurdish peshmerga expand to fill void → Sunni population perceives ethnic threat → Sunni fighters join insurgency as both economic actors and sectarian defenders (two independent recruitment pathways reinforcing each other) Phase 4: External involvement : Instability → Al-Qaeda in Iraq enters → foreign fighters provide financing and ideological framing → insurgency becomes internationalized → coalition military response intensifies → civilian casualties → insurgent recruitment further increases (another reinforcing loop) ::: ::: The system dynamics perspective reveals that the insurgency was not primarily caused by the presence of foreign fighters or by "bad actors" who could have been identified in advance — it was produced by the institutional architecture of the intervention itself. A systems analysis conducted before Order No. 2 could have identified these cascading pathways. --- # Part X: Tipping Points and Threshold Dynamics ## When Systems Shift Suddenly Some of the most important outcomes in international security are characterized by sudden, discontinuous change — the collapse of a regime that appeared stable, the rapid escalation of a conflict that seemed contained, the sudden spread of a protest movement. Systems thinking offers a framework for understanding why these transitions happen. **Key concept: tipping points and bifurcations** In a system with strong balancing loops, a variable (say, regime stability) may remain near its equilibrium despite perturbations — the system "absorbs" small shocks. But once a critical threshold is crossed, the system reorganizes abruptly. The balancing loops that maintained the old equilibrium weaken or reverse; reinforcing loops that were previously dominated begin to dominate. The system bifurcates to a new state. **Illustrative cases:** | Phenomenon | Pre-tip Dynamics | Tipping Mechanism | Post-tip Dynamics | |---|---|---|---| | Arab Spring 2010–11 | Authoritarian stability through repression (balancing loop) | Mohamed Bouazizi self-immolation → social media amplification of grievance → cost of protest fell below cost of silence | Reinforcing loop: protests → government crackdowns → more protests; cascade across regional states | | Soviet collapse 1989–91 | Ideological legitimacy + military deterrence maintained stability | Gorbachev's signals that force would not be used → cost of defection fell for Eastern European elites | Cascade: each defection reduced the expected cost of the next; the system reorganized faster than anyone predicted | | Financial contagion 2008 | Mortgage-backed securities held by globally interconnected financial institutions | Lehman Brothers failure → counterparty uncertainty → credit markets freeze | Reinforcing loop: credit freeze → institutional failures → more uncertainty → deeper freeze | ::: {.callout-warning} ## The Prediction Problem Tipping points are extraordinarily difficult to predict precisely because the systems that generate them appear stable until they are not. Leading indicators exist — accelerating protest frequency before regime collapse, rising volatility before financial crisis — but threshold identification requires continuous system monitoring, not periodic snapshot analysis. Intelligence that reports on what a system *is* without modeling where it is relative to its thresholds will miss the discontinuity. ::: --- # Part XI: What Is Agent-Based Modeling? Systems thinking and causal loop diagrams (Part VIII) describe a system's structure qualitatively — they map how variables push on one another. But they stop short of *generating* the system's behavior. **Agent-based modeling (ABM)** closes that gap. It is a computational method that builds a system from the bottom up: rather than writing equations for aggregate quantities, the analyst specifies the individual actors, gives each a small set of behavioral rules, lets them interact in an environment, and observes what population-level patterns emerge. An agent-based model is a simulation composed of many autonomous *agents* — people, households, military units, states, firms, vehicles — each following simple rules and interacting over time. The model is run forward, and the analyst studies the macro-level patterns that arise from the accumulated micro-level interactions. ::: {.callout-note} ## The Core Move — Bottom-Up Explanation Most statistical models are *top-down*: they fit an aggregate relationship (e.g., "unemployment predicts unrest") without specifying the mechanism that produces it. ABM is *bottom-up*: it asks what individual behavior, repeated across thousands of agents, would *generate* that aggregate relationship. If simple, plausible individual rules reproduce the observed macro-pattern, the model offers a candidate explanation — what Joshua Epstein calls *generative* social science: "If you didn't grow it, you didn't explain it." ::: Two broad modeling philosophies sit in contrast: ::: {.col-pair} ::: {.col-left} **Equation-Based / Aggregate Models** Track population-level variables directly (stocks, rates, averages). Assume homogeneity or represent the "average" actor. Computationally efficient and analytically tractable, but they hide heterogeneity, local interaction, and the possibility that individuals differ in ways that matter. ::: ::: {.col-right} **Agent-Based Models** Track heterogeneous individuals and their interactions explicitly. Emergence, thresholds, and spatial or network structure arise naturally from the simulation. The cost: models are heavier to build, harder to calibrate, and must be run many times to characterize their behavior. ::: ::: --- # Part XII: Anatomy of an Agent-Based Model Every ABM, however elaborate, is assembled from the same handful of components. ::: {.key-terms} ::: {dl} Agents : The autonomous entities. Each has *attributes* (state variables: location, resources, beliefs, health, allegiance) and *behaviors* (rules mapping its situation to an action). Agents are typically *heterogeneous* — they differ in attributes and sometimes in the rules they follow. Environment : The space in which agents live and interact: a grid, a geographic map, a social network, or an abstract landscape. The environment may hold its own state (terrain, resources, infrastructure) and change over time. Behavioral Rules : Condition–action statements that govern each agent ("if fewer than one-third of my neighbors are like me, relocate"). Rules are usually *simple* and *local* — agents act on nearby, partial information, not a global view of the system. Interactions : How agents affect one another and the environment: they compete, imitate, transmit, attack, trade, or communicate. The *interaction structure* — who can influence whom — is set by the environment or an underlying network. Scheduling / Time : The model advances in discrete steps ("ticks"). At each step, agents are activated — synchronously, or one at a time in random order — and update their states. Activation order can itself affect outcomes. Stochasticity : Randomness in initial conditions and in rules. Because outcomes vary from run to run, an ABM is executed many times and analyzed as a *distribution* of outcomes, never a single trajectory. ::: ::: ::: {.callout-tip} ## Reading the Required Reading Through This Lens "Parable of the Polygons" is a complete agent-based model. The *agents* are the shapes; the *environment* is the grid; the *rule* is "move if too few of my neighbors are like me"; the *interaction* is neighborhood adjacency; and the *emergent pattern* is segregation. When you drag the bias slider and re-run, you are doing sensitivity analysis by hand. ::: --- # Part XIII: From Micro-Rules to Macro-Patterns The payoff of ABM is **emergence** — system-level patterns that no single agent intends or contains. The canonical demonstration is Thomas Schelling's segregation model (1971). Place two groups of agents on a grid. Each agent is *content* as long as some modest fraction of its immediate neighbors shares its type; if not, it relocates to a nearby empty cell. Crucially, no agent seeks segregation — an agent can be perfectly happy as a local minority (content, say, when only 30 percent of its neighbors match). Yet run the model forward and the grid reliably self-organizes into starkly segregated clusters. The macro-outcome is far more extreme than any micro-preference. > The analytic lesson is blunt: **you cannot read collective outcomes off individual intentions.** A segregated city is not proof its residents are extremists; a stable regime is not proof of deep loyalty; a viral rumor is not proof of coordinated deception. The pattern lives in the *interactions*, not in any one agent's mind. This is the same warning Part X raised about tipping points — now equipped with an explicit mechanism. Where a causal loop diagram *asserts* that feedback exists, an ABM *produces* the feedback and lets the analyst watch it tip. ::: {.col-pair} ::: {.col-left} **Causal Loop Diagram (Part VIII)** A qualitative map of feedback structure. Excellent for framing a problem, surfacing assumptions, and communicating with consumers. It does not, by itself, generate quantitative, temporal, or spatial dynamics. ::: ::: {.col-right} **Agent-Based Model** An executable model that *generates* behavior. It can reproduce thresholds, cascades, and spatial patterns — but it demands that every rule be specified precisely, and its output must be validated against something in the real world. ::: ::: --- # Part XIV: Canonical Models Every Analyst Should Recognize A small set of classic models recurs across disciplines. Recognizing them gives an analyst a vocabulary for spotting the same dynamics in intelligence problems. | Model | Agents & Core Rule | What It Demonstrates | Intelligence Analogy | |---|---|---|---| | **Schelling segregation** | Shapes on a grid; relocate if too few like-neighbors | Mild individual preference → extreme collective sorting | Ethnic enclaving; online self-segregation and echo chambers | | **Boids** (Reynolds) | "Birds" obey separation, alignment, cohesion | Lifelike flocking from three local rules | Crowd and convoy movement; drone swarms | | **Axelrod's tournament** | Strategies play repeated Prisoner's Dilemma | Reciprocity (tit-for-tat) can emerge and stabilize cooperation | Alliance durability; deterrence; informant–insurgent dynamics | | **Sugarscape** (Epstein & Axtell) | Agents harvest a renewable resource on a landscape | Inequality, migration, and trade emerge from simple foraging | Resource competition; migration and displacement | | **Epidemic (SIR-type)** | Agents infect neighbors probabilistically | Thresholds, super-spreaders, network effects | Contagion of disease, rumor, panic, or radicalization | --- # Part XV: Agent-Based Modeling in Intelligence Analysis ABM is attractive to the intelligence analyst precisely because so many national-security problems are *emergent*: the outcome that matters (an insurgency, a bank run, a protest wave) is produced by many interacting decisions rather than dictated from the top. Representative applications include: - **Conflict and insurgency dynamics** — recruitment, defection, and violence as products of local grievance and peer behavior - **Protest and mobilization cascades** — when the cost of joining falls below the cost of staying home for enough people at once - **Radicalization and opinion diffusion** — how beliefs, rumors, and disinformation spread across a social network - **Epidemic and CBRN spread** — contagion and contamination through contact and movement networks - **Logistics and supply chains** — cascading disruption from a single failed node - **Wargaming and red-teaming** — simulating adversary decision-making under alternative assumptions - **Crowd behavior and evacuation** — movement, congestion, and panic in confined spaces - **Illicit and adaptive networks** — how trafficking or proliferation networks reconstitute after nodes are removed ::: {.callout-note} ## Worked Example — Modeling the Iraq Recruitment Loop Part IX described the post-2003 insurgency as an emergent product of institutional collapse. ABM lets us make that argument *executable*. Represent each former soldier as an agent with a "join the insurgency" threshold that falls as (a) local grievance rises and (b) more neighbors have already joined. Seed the model with the shock of Order No. 2 — 400,000 newly unemployed, armed agents — and the reinforcing recruitment loop reproduces a rapid tipping into widespread insurgency. The value is not the exact numbers but the *counterfactual experiment*: re-run the model **without** disbanding the army, or with a phased demobilization, and compare the trajectories. ABM turns "what if we had sequenced this differently?" into a question the analyst can actually explore on a synthetic society. ::: --- # Part XVI: Building and Validating an Agent-Based Model ::: {.timeline} ::: {dl} Step 1: Define the Question and Scope : State precisely what pattern or decision the model must illuminate. The right level of detail is the *minimum* needed to address the question — not the most realistic model imaginable. Step 2: Specify Agents, Attributes, and Rules : Decide who the agents are, what state they carry, and what simple, local rules drive their behavior. Document every rule explicitly. Step 3: Define the Environment and Interactions : Choose the space (grid, map, network) and the interaction structure — who can affect whom, and how. Step 4: Set Initial Conditions and Parameters : Establish the starting population, its distribution, and the parameter values. Where possible, ground these in real data. Step 5: Run and Explore : Execute the model many times with different random seeds. Because outcomes are stochastic, study the *distribution* of results, not a single run. Step 6: Verify, Then Validate : *Verification* asks "did I build the model right?" — does the code do what I intended? *Validation* asks "did I build the right model?" — does its output match real-world data or well-established stylized facts? Step 7: Sensitivity Analysis : Vary parameters and rules to learn which ones drive the outcome and where the tipping thresholds lie. This is often the most analytically valuable step — it reveals the leverage points (Part on Key Principles) of the modeled system. ::: ::: Common platforms include **NetLogo** (the standard teaching and prototyping tool), **Mesa** (a Python framework), **Repast**, **GAMA**, and **AnyLogic**. The tool matters far less than the discipline of the workflow above. ::: {.callout-warning} ## Equifinality — The Trap of the Convincing Match Many *different* rule-sets can produce the *same* macro-pattern. Reproducing segregation, or an insurgency curve, does **not** prove your assumed mechanism is the real one — only that it is *sufficient* to generate the pattern. Validation against a single output is weak evidence; matching multiple independent features of the real system ("pattern-oriented modeling") is stronger. An ABM builds confidence in a *mechanism*; it does not confirm it. ::: --- # Part XVII: Strengths, Limitations, and Pitfalls ::: {.col-pair} ::: {.col-left} **Strengths** - Captures **heterogeneity** and **local interaction** that aggregate models average away - Naturally produces **emergence**, **thresholds**, and **cascades** - Enables **controlled experiments** and **counterfactuals** impossible in the real world - Integrates **spatial** and **network** structure directly - Forces **mechanistic transparency** — every assumption is written as a rule ::: ::: {.col-right} **Limitations & Pitfalls** - **Hard to calibrate and validate** against real data - **Sensitive** to rule and parameter choices; small changes can flip outcomes - **Equifinality** — the right pattern can arise from the wrong mechanism - **Computationally expensive** at realistic scale - **False precision** — polished animations imply more certainty than warranted ::: ::: ::: {.callout-warning} ## The False-Precision Trap A colorful, animated simulation projecting a specific outcome can imply far more certainty than the model earns. Communicate ABM output as a *distribution of possibilities* and an *exploration of mechanisms* — never a point forecast. This is the same discipline demanded by the tipping-point and probability material earlier in the course: model the uncertainty, and say so plainly to the consumer. ::: ::: {.callout-note} ## Keep It Simple The goal of an agent-based model is the *simplest* set of rules that generates the pattern of interest — not the most detailed replica of reality. A model complex enough to reproduce everything explains nothing, because the analyst can no longer see which assumptions are doing the work. ::: --- # Suggested Readings Haggard, S., & Noland, M. (2010). Sanctioning North Korea: The political economy of denuclearization and proliferation. *Asian Survey*, *50*(3), 539–568. [Access via the JMU Library](https://search.lib.jmu.edu/permalink/01JMU_INST/14emilk/cdi_proquest_miscellaneous_754067446) > Empirical examination of feedback dynamics in the North Korea sanctions case — directly relevant to the systems thinking case studies in this lecture. Cederman, L. E. (2003). Modeling the size of wars: From billiard balls to sandpiles. *American Political Science Review*, *97*(1), 135–150. [Access via the JMU Library](https://search.lib.jmu.edu/permalink/01JMU_INST/14emilk/cdi_proquest_miscellaneous_60644309) > Applies complex systems and self-organized criticality to war size distributions. Formal demonstration that conflict systems exhibit tipping point dynamics inconsistent with standard linear models. Schelling, T. C. (1971). Dynamic models of segregation. *Journal of Mathematical Sociology*, *1*(2), 143–186. [Access via the JMU Library](https://search.lib.jmu.edu/permalink/01JMU_INST/14emilk/cdi_econis_primary_486241769) > The founding agent-based segregation model. Shows formally how mild individual preferences generate extreme collective sorting — the emergence result behind this week's required reading. Bonabeau, E. (2002). Agent-based modeling: Methods and techniques for simulating human systems. *Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences*, *99*(suppl. 3), 7280–7287. [Access via the JMU Library](https://search.lib.jmu.edu/permalink/01JMU_INST/14emilk/cdi_proquest_journals_201386832) > Accessible overview of when and why to use ABM for human systems, including its advantages over aggregate models and its practical limitations. Epstein, J. M. (1999). Agent-based computational models and generative social science. *Complexity*, *4*(5), 41–60. [Access via the JMU Library](https://search.lib.jmu.edu/permalink/01JMU_INST/14emilk/cdi_crossref_primary_10_1002__SICI_1099_0526_199905_06_4_5_41__AID_CPLX9_3_0_CO_2_F) > The statement of the "generative" approach — "if you didn't grow it, you didn't explain it" — that frames Part XI's bottom-up view of explanation.

Mechanistic specificity is a hallmark of analytically rigorous hypothesis development. ::: ::: --- # Part IX: Real-World Systems Thinking Cases ## Case 1: Sanctions Against North Korea — A Feedback Loop Analysis Sanctions imposed on North Korea to compel denuclearization have operated for decades with limited observable effect on North Korea's weapons programs. Systems thinking explains why. The causal structure contains multiple feedback loops that counteract the intended effect of economic coercion. **Intended mechanism (the linear assumption):** > Sanctions → Economic pain → Regime capitulation → Denuclearization **What systems analysis reveals:** ::: {.col-pair} ::: {.col-left} **Reinforcing loop — Rally-around-the-flag** Sanctions-induced economic hardship → Regime frames hardship as foreign aggression → Domestic nationalism increases → Regime legitimacy strengthens → Hardline position becomes domestically necessary to sustain This reinforcing loop amplifies regime cohesion in direct response to the pressure intended to weaken it. Empirical studies on North Korea have found that periods of intensified sanctions correspond with increased domestic propaganda output, not capitulation signals. ::: ::: {.col-right} **Balancing loop — Elite defection risk** Sanctions → Economic contraction → Elite material wellbeing threatened → Elite defection or coup risk → Regime redistributes resources to core loyalists → Economic contraction among population deepens, but regime stability is maintained This balancing loop absorbs the potential political threat from sanctions by concentrating resources within the elite network—precisely the opposite of the intended distributional effect. ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note} ## Analytical Takeaway A linear model of sanctions predicts capitulation. A systems model predicts adaptation and consolidation—which is what the empirical record shows. Before recommending or evaluating a coercive policy instrument, mapping the full causal structure (including second- and third-order loops) is essential analytical due diligence. ::: ## Case 2: The 2003 Iraq Occupation — Institutional Collapse as a System Failure The postwar period in Iraq demonstrated how policy interventions can trigger cascading system failures through mechanisms that linear planning does not anticipate. **The critical decision:** Coalition Provisional Authority Order No. 2 (May 2003) disbanded the Iraqi Army, removing approximately 400,000 armed men from institutional employment overnight. **System consequences:** ::: {.timeline} ::: {dl} Phase 1: Initial shock : Army dissolution → immediate loss of income and status for 400,000 individuals with weapons training → security vacuum as the primary order-providing institution disappears Phase 2: Grievance amplification (reinforcing loop) : Unemployed soldiers → economic desperation + institutional grievance → insurgent recruitment becomes easy and cheap → insurgency capability increases Phase 3: Sectarian activation : Security vacuum → Shia militias and Kurdish peshmerga expand to fill void → Sunni population perceives ethnic threat → Sunni fighters join insurgency as both economic actors and sectarian defenders (two independent recruitment pathways reinforcing each other) Phase 4: External involvement : Instability → Al-Qaeda in Iraq enters → foreign fighters provide financing and ideological framing → insurgency becomes internationalized → coalition military response intensifies → civilian casualties → insurgent recruitment further increases (another reinforcing loop) ::: ::: The system dynamics perspective reveals that the insurgency was not primarily caused by the presence of foreign fighters or by "bad actors" who could have been identified in advance — it was produced by the institutional architecture of the intervention itself. A systems analysis conducted before Order No. 2 could have identified these cascading pathways. --- # Part X: Tipping Points and Threshold Dynamics ## When Systems Shift Suddenly Some of the most important outcomes in international security are characterized by sudden, discontinuous change — the collapse of a regime that appeared stable, the rapid escalation of a conflict that seemed contained, the sudden spread of a protest movement. Systems thinking offers a framework for understanding why these transitions happen. **Key concept: tipping points and bifurcations** In a system with strong balancing loops, a variable (say, regime stability) may remain near its equilibrium despite perturbations — the system "absorbs" small shocks. But once a critical threshold is crossed, the system reorganizes abruptly. The balancing loops that maintained the old equilibrium weaken or reverse; reinforcing loops that were previously dominated begin to dominate. The system bifurcates to a new state. **Illustrative cases:** | Phenomenon | Pre-tip Dynamics | Tipping Mechanism | Post-tip Dynamics | |---|---|---|---| | Arab Spring 2010–11 | Authoritarian stability through repression (balancing loop) | Mohamed Bouazizi self-immolation → social media amplification of grievance → cost of protest fell below cost of silence | Reinforcing loop: protests → government crackdowns → more protests; cascade across regional states | | Soviet collapse 1989–91 | Ideological legitimacy + military deterrence maintained stability | Gorbachev's signals that force would not be used → cost of defection fell for Eastern European elites | Cascade: each defection reduced the expected cost of the next; the system reorganized faster than anyone predicted | | Financial contagion 2008 | Mortgage-backed securities held by globally interconnected financial institutions | Lehman Brothers failure → counterparty uncertainty → credit markets freeze | Reinforcing loop: credit freeze → institutional failures → more uncertainty → deeper freeze | ::: {.callout-warning} ## The Prediction Problem Tipping points are extraordinarily difficult to predict precisely because the systems that generate them appear stable until they are not. Leading indicators exist — accelerating protest frequency before regime collapse, rising volatility before financial crisis — but threshold identification requires continuous system monitoring, not periodic snapshot analysis. Intelligence that reports on what a system *is* without modeling where it is relative to its thresholds will miss the discontinuity. ::: --- # Part XI: What Is Agent-Based Modeling? Systems thinking and causal loop diagrams (Part VIII) describe a system's structure qualitatively — they map how variables push on one another. But they stop short of *generating* the system's behavior. **Agent-based modeling (ABM)** closes that gap. It is a computational method that builds a system from the bottom up: rather than writing equations for aggregate quantities, the analyst specifies the individual actors, gives each a small set of behavioral rules, lets them interact in an environment, and observes what population-level patterns emerge. An agent-based model is a simulation composed of many autonomous *agents* — people, households, military units, states, firms, vehicles — each following simple rules and interacting over time. The model is run forward, and the analyst studies the macro-level patterns that arise from the accumulated micro-level interactions. ::: {.callout-note} ## The Core Move — Bottom-Up Explanation Most statistical models are *top-down*: they fit an aggregate relationship (e.g., "unemployment predicts unrest") without specifying the mechanism that produces it. ABM is *bottom-up*: it asks what individual behavior, repeated across thousands of agents, would *generate* that aggregate relationship. If simple, plausible individual rules reproduce the observed macro-pattern, the model offers a candidate explanation — what Joshua Epstein calls *generative* social science: "If you didn't grow it, you didn't explain it." ::: Two broad modeling philosophies sit in contrast: ::: {.col-pair} ::: {.col-left} **Equation-Based / Aggregate Models** Track population-level variables directly (stocks, rates, averages). Assume homogeneity or represent the "average" actor. Computationally efficient and analytically tractable, but they hide heterogeneity, local interaction, and the possibility that individuals differ in ways that matter. ::: ::: {.col-right} **Agent-Based Models** Track heterogeneous individuals and their interactions explicitly. Emergence, thresholds, and spatial or network structure arise naturally from the simulation. The cost: models are heavier to build, harder to calibrate, and must be run many times to characterize their behavior. ::: ::: --- # Part XII: Anatomy of an Agent-Based Model Every ABM, however elaborate, is assembled from the same handful of components. ::: {.key-terms} ::: {dl} Agents : The autonomous entities. Each has *attributes* (state variables: location, resources, beliefs, health, allegiance) and *behaviors* (rules mapping its situation to an action). Agents are typically *heterogeneous* — they differ in attributes and sometimes in the rules they follow. Environment : The space in which agents live and interact: a grid, a geographic map, a social network, or an abstract landscape. The environment may hold its own state (terrain, resources, infrastructure) and change over time. Behavioral Rules : Condition–action statements that govern each agent ("if fewer than one-third of my neighbors are like me, relocate"). Rules are usually *simple* and *local* — agents act on nearby, partial information, not a global view of the system. Interactions : How agents affect one another and the environment: they compete, imitate, transmit, attack, trade, or communicate. The *interaction structure* — who can influence whom — is set by the environment or an underlying network. Scheduling / Time : The model advances in discrete steps ("ticks"). At each step, agents are activated — synchronously, or one at a time in random order — and update their states. Activation order can itself affect outcomes. Stochasticity : Randomness in initial conditions and in rules. Because outcomes vary from run to run, an ABM is executed many times and analyzed as a *distribution* of outcomes, never a single trajectory. ::: ::: ::: {.callout-tip} ## Reading the Required Reading Through This Lens "Parable of the Polygons" is a complete agent-based model. The *agents* are the shapes; the *environment* is the grid; the *rule* is "move if too few of my neighbors are like me"; the *interaction* is neighborhood adjacency; and the *emergent pattern* is segregation. When you drag the bias slider and re-run, you are doing sensitivity analysis by hand. ::: --- # Part XIII: From Micro-Rules to Macro-Patterns The payoff of ABM is **emergence** — system-level patterns that no single agent intends or contains. The canonical demonstration is Thomas Schelling's segregation model (1971). Place two groups of agents on a grid. Each agent is *content* as long as some modest fraction of its immediate neighbors shares its type; if not, it relocates to a nearby empty cell. Crucially, no agent seeks segregation — an agent can be perfectly happy as a local minority (content, say, when only 30 percent of its neighbors match). Yet run the model forward and the grid reliably self-organizes into starkly segregated clusters. The macro-outcome is far more extreme than any micro-preference. > The analytic lesson is blunt: **you cannot read collective outcomes off individual intentions.** A segregated city is not proof its residents are extremists; a stable regime is not proof of deep loyalty; a viral rumor is not proof of coordinated deception. The pattern lives in the *interactions*, not in any one agent's mind. This is the same warning Part X raised about tipping points — now equipped with an explicit mechanism. Where a causal loop diagram *asserts* that feedback exists, an ABM *produces* the feedback and lets the analyst watch it tip. ::: {.col-pair} ::: {.col-left} **Causal Loop Diagram (Part VIII)** A qualitative map of feedback structure. Excellent for framing a problem, surfacing assumptions, and communicating with consumers. It does not, by itself, generate quantitative, temporal, or spatial dynamics. ::: ::: {.col-right} **Agent-Based Model** An executable model that *generates* behavior. It can reproduce thresholds, cascades, and spatial patterns — but it demands that every rule be specified precisely, and its output must be validated against something in the real world. ::: ::: --- # Part XIV: Canonical Models Every Analyst Should Recognize A small set of classic models recurs across disciplines. Recognizing them gives an analyst a vocabulary for spotting the same dynamics in intelligence problems. | Model | Agents & Core Rule | What It Demonstrates | Intelligence Analogy | |---|---|---|---| | **Schelling segregation** | Shapes on a grid; relocate if too few like-neighbors | Mild individual preference → extreme collective sorting | Ethnic enclaving; online self-segregation and echo chambers | | **Boids** (Reynolds) | "Birds" obey separation, alignment, cohesion | Lifelike flocking from three local rules | Crowd and convoy movement; drone swarms | | **Axelrod's tournament** | Strategies play repeated Prisoner's Dilemma | Reciprocity (tit-for-tat) can emerge and stabilize cooperation | Alliance durability; deterrence; informant–insurgent dynamics | | **Sugarscape** (Epstein & Axtell) | Agents harvest a renewable resource on a landscape | Inequality, migration, and trade emerge from simple foraging | Resource competition; migration and displacement | | **Epidemic (SIR-type)** | Agents infect neighbors probabilistically | Thresholds, super-spreaders, network effects | Contagion of disease, rumor, panic, or radicalization | --- # Part XV: Agent-Based Modeling in Intelligence Analysis ABM is attractive to the intelligence analyst precisely because so many national-security problems are *emergent*: the outcome that matters (an insurgency, a bank run, a protest wave) is produced by many interacting decisions rather than dictated from the top. Representative applications include: - **Conflict and insurgency dynamics** — recruitment, defection, and violence as products of local grievance and peer behavior - **Protest and mobilization cascades** — when the cost of joining falls below the cost of staying home for enough people at once - **Radicalization and opinion diffusion** — how beliefs, rumors, and disinformation spread across a social network - **Epidemic and CBRN spread** — contagion and contamination through contact and movement networks - **Logistics and supply chains** — cascading disruption from a single failed node - **Wargaming and red-teaming** — simulating adversary decision-making under alternative assumptions - **Crowd behavior and evacuation** — movement, congestion, and panic in confined spaces - **Illicit and adaptive networks** — how trafficking or proliferation networks reconstitute after nodes are removed ::: {.callout-note} ## Worked Example — Modeling the Iraq Recruitment Loop Part IX described the post-2003 insurgency as an emergent product of institutional collapse. ABM lets us make that argument *executable*. Represent each former soldier as an agent with a "join the insurgency" threshold that falls as (a) local grievance rises and (b) more neighbors have already joined. Seed the model with the shock of Order No. 2 — 400,000 newly unemployed, armed agents — and the reinforcing recruitment loop reproduces a rapid tipping into widespread insurgency. The value is not the exact numbers but the *counterfactual experiment*: re-run the model **without** disbanding the army, or with a phased demobilization, and compare the trajectories. ABM turns "what if we had sequenced this differently?" into a question the analyst can actually explore on a synthetic society. ::: --- # Part XVI: Building and Validating an Agent-Based Model ::: {.timeline} ::: {dl} Step 1: Define the Question and Scope : State precisely what pattern or decision the model must illuminate. The right level of detail is the *minimum* needed to address the question — not the most realistic model imaginable. Step 2: Specify Agents, Attributes, and Rules : Decide who the agents are, what state they carry, and what simple, local rules drive their behavior. Document every rule explicitly. Step 3: Define the Environment and Interactions : Choose the space (grid, map, network) and the interaction structure — who can affect whom, and how. Step 4: Set Initial Conditions and Parameters : Establish the starting population, its distribution, and the parameter values. Where possible, ground these in real data. Step 5: Run and Explore : Execute the model many times with different random seeds. Because outcomes are stochastic, study the *distribution* of results, not a single run. Step 6: Verify, Then Validate : *Verification* asks "did I build the model right?" — does the code do what I intended? *Validation* asks "did I build the right model?" — does its output match real-world data or well-established stylized facts? Step 7: Sensitivity Analysis : Vary parameters and rules to learn which ones drive the outcome and where the tipping thresholds lie. This is often the most analytically valuable step — it reveals the leverage points (Part on Key Principles) of the modeled system. ::: ::: Common platforms include **NetLogo** (the standard teaching and prototyping tool), **Mesa** (a Python framework), **Repast**, **GAMA**, and **AnyLogic**. The tool matters far less than the discipline of the workflow above. ::: {.callout-warning} ## Equifinality — The Trap of the Convincing Match Many *different* rule-sets can produce the *same* macro-pattern. Reproducing segregation, or an insurgency curve, does **not** prove your assumed mechanism is the real one — only that it is *sufficient* to generate the pattern. Validation against a single output is weak evidence; matching multiple independent features of the real system ("pattern-oriented modeling") is stronger. An ABM builds confidence in a *mechanism*; it does not confirm it. ::: --- # Part XVII: Strengths, Limitations, and Pitfalls ::: {.col-pair} ::: {.col-left} **Strengths** - Captures **heterogeneity** and **local interaction** that aggregate models average away - Naturally produces **emergence**, **thresholds**, and **cascades** - Enables **controlled experiments** and **counterfactuals** impossible in the real world - Integrates **spatial** and **network** structure directly - Forces **mechanistic transparency** — every assumption is written as a rule ::: ::: {.col-right} **Limitations & Pitfalls** - **Hard to calibrate and validate** against real data - **Sensitive** to rule and parameter choices; small changes can flip outcomes - **Equifinality** — the right pattern can arise from the wrong mechanism - **Computationally expensive** at realistic scale - **False precision** — polished animations imply more certainty than warranted ::: ::: ::: {.callout-warning} ## The False-Precision Trap A colorful, animated simulation projecting a specific outcome can imply far more certainty than the model earns. Communicate ABM output as a *distribution of possibilities* and an *exploration of mechanisms* — never a point forecast. This is the same discipline demanded by the tipping-point and probability material earlier in the course: model the uncertainty, and say so plainly to the consumer. ::: ::: {.callout-note} ## Keep It Simple The goal of an agent-based model is the *simplest* set of rules that generates the pattern of interest — not the most detailed replica of reality. A model complex enough to reproduce everything explains nothing, because the analyst can no longer see which assumptions are doing the work. ::: --- # Suggested Readings Haggard, S., & Noland, M. (2010). Sanctioning North Korea: The political economy of denuclearization and proliferation. *Asian Survey*, *50*(3), 539–568. [Access via the JMU Library](https://search.lib.jmu.edu/permalink/01JMU_INST/14emilk/cdi_proquest_miscellaneous_754067446) > Empirical examination of feedback dynamics in the North Korea sanctions case — directly relevant to the systems thinking case studies in this lecture. Cederman, L. E. (2003). Modeling the size of wars: From billiard balls to sandpiles. *American Political Science Review*, *97*(1), 135–150. [Access via the JMU Library](https://search.lib.jmu.edu/permalink/01JMU_INST/14emilk/cdi_proquest_miscellaneous_60644309) > Applies complex systems and self-organized criticality to war size distributions. Formal demonstration that conflict systems exhibit tipping point dynamics inconsistent with standard linear models. Schelling, T. C. (1971). Dynamic models of segregation. *Journal of Mathematical Sociology*, *1*(2), 143–186. [Access via the JMU Library](https://search.lib.jmu.edu/permalink/01JMU_INST/14emilk/cdi_econis_primary_486241769) > The founding agent-based segregation model. Shows formally how mild individual preferences generate extreme collective sorting — the emergence result behind this week's required reading. Bonabeau, E. (2002). Agent-based modeling: Methods and techniques for simulating human systems. *Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences*, *99*(suppl. 3), 7280–7287. [Access via the JMU Library](https://search.lib.jmu.edu/permalink/01JMU_INST/14emilk/cdi_proquest_journals_201386832) > Accessible overview of when and why to use ABM for human systems, including its advantages over aggregate models and its practical limitations. Epstein, J. M. (1999). Agent-based computational models and generative social science. *Complexity*, *4*(5), 41–60. [Access via the JMU Library](https://search.lib.jmu.edu/permalink/01JMU_INST/14emilk/cdi_crossref_primary_10_1002__SICI_1099_0526_199905_06_4_5_41__AID_CPLX9_3_0_CO_2_F) > The statement of the "generative" approach — "if you didn't grow it, you didn't explain it" — that frames Part XI's bottom-up view of explanation.
